Title: SUCROSE TRANSPORTER 5 supplies Arabidopsis embryos with biotin and affects triacylglycerol accumulation

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Biotin is an essential cofactor for fatty acid synthesis; triacylglycerol is the major seed oil storage reserve supporting embryo/seedling development
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SUC5 promotes biotin import into developing embryos and thereby supports normal triacylglycerol accumulation and fatty acid composition under biotin-limiting conditions; suc5 alone reduces embryo biotin uptake but does not noticeably alter seed TAG under normal conditions
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant analysis (suc5.4, suc5.5); radiolabeled [14C]-biotin and [14C]-sucrose uptake assays in isolated embryos; promoter-GFP expression analysis; GFP fusion subcellular localization; genetic interaction with bio1.1 and bio2.1 double mutants; seed TAG and fatty acid profiling; biotin supplementation rescue
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in endosperm and, at later stages, in the epidermis of torpedo-stage and older embryos; peak expression around 7 DAF
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Endosperm and embryo epidermis, especially outer cotyledon epidermis during late embryo development
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: SUC/SUT sucrose transporter family, SUT1 clade; predicted 12-transmembrane H+/solute symporter
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Biotin supplementation (0, 0.1, 1 mM); developing seeds/embryos at 7–8 DAF
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtSUC5 is localized to the plasma membrane and expressed in developing seed tissues, including embryo epidermis. 2. Loss of AtSUC5 significantly reduces biotin uptake into isolated embryos but does not reduce sucrose uptake, showing its physiological role as a biotin transporter in planta. 3. In bio1 or bio2 biotin-biosynthesis mutant backgrounds, loss of AtSUC5 strongly aggravates embryo/seed defects and further lowers seed triacylglycerol content, demonstrating that AtSUC5-mediated biotin supply supports fatty acid and TAG biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: suc5 single mutants show little visible phenotype under normal conditions, implying conditional importance mainly under biotin limitation; in bio mutant backgrounds loss of SUC5 causes severe seed developmental defects and low seed oil
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native SUC5 promoter (2030 bp) for reporter constructs; constitutive transient expression vectors for GFP-SUC5/SUC5-GFP localization
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although SUC5 is a classical sucrose/H+ symporter in heterologous systems, in developing Arabidopsis embryos its physiologically demonstrated critical role is biotin transport rather than sucrose transport; other SUC transporters may compensate for sucrose import. The manuscript title and results identify SUC5 as the central experimental gene affecting nutrient metabolism.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Biotin is required as a cofactor for fatty acid synthesis; TAG is seed storage oil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BIO1 is required for endogenous biotin biosynthesis; bio1 mutants have impaired embryo/seedling development and strongly reduced seed TAG, and defects are further enhanced by suc5 mutation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Single-mutant and double-mutant phenotypic comparison with suc5; biotin supplementation rescue; seed TAG and fatty acid analysis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bio1.1 mutants show biotin-dependent seedling and seed defects with severe reduction of seed TAG. 2. Combining bio1.1 with suc5.4 further aggravates developmental defects and lowers TAG content. 3. Exogenous biotin partially or substantially rescues these phenotypes, consistent with BIO1 acting in biotin biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of BIO1 causes poor germination, pale/tiny seedlings, reduced seed weight and oil accumulation
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BIO1 is a core experimental comparator because the paper tests SUC5 function in the context of biotin-biosynthesis deficiency; however, the paper is not primarily a mechanistic study of BIO1 itself.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Biotin is required as a cofactor for fatty acid synthesis; TAG is seed storage oil
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BIO2 is required for endogenous biotin biosynthesis; bio2 mutants show reduced embryo development, wrinkled seeds, reduced TAG and altered fatty acid composition, and these defects are strongly enhanced by suc5 mutation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Single-mutant and double-mutant phenotypic comparison with suc5; biotin supplementation rescue; seed TAG and fatty acid analysis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bio2.1 mutants show biotin-dependent defects in seed and embryo development and lower seed TAG with altered fatty acid composition. 2. bio2.1 suc5.5 double mutants display much stronger defects, indicating that SUC5-mediated biotin transport and BIO2-mediated biotin synthesis jointly support embryo biotin supply. 3. External biotin rescues the phenotype incompletely in double mutants, supporting transport limitation in the absence of SUC5.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of BIO2 causes poor growth on biotin-free medium, wrinkled/low-weight seeds, and severe reduction in seed oil
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: BIO2 is used as a key genetic background to reveal the nutrient-metabolic function of SUC5; the paper does not provide detailed mechanistic characterization of BIO2 enzyme activity.
